title: Implement project setup decisions from PROJECT_DECISIONS.md
description: Track and implement all concrete setup work implied by decisions in PROJECT_DECISIONS.md, including repo/docs, directory layout, branching & collaboration settings, writing conventions, research/data guidance, bibliography, frontmatter template, PR template, and automation/tooling.

title: Create /articles/ directory
description: Create the /articles/ directory for all article content. Series get subdirectories (e.g. /articles/ai-ethics-series/part-1/). Standalone articles sit directly under /articles/.

title: Create /assets/ directory
description: Create the /assets/ directory for images, figures, and charts.

title: Create references.bib
description: Create shared bibliography file (BibTeX) at repo root. Key format: first four letters of surname + last two digits of year (e.g. Smit24).

title: Create PR template with editorial checklist
description: Create .github/pull_request_template.md with lightweight editorial checklist: Summary, Type, and checklist items (one sentence per line, frontmatter complete, research linked, spellcheck done).

title: Create CODEOWNERS file
description: Create .github/CODEOWNERS to require owner review on all PRs to main. Per Decision #5, owner is Integration Manager with final merge authority.

title: Set up branch protection rules on main
description: Configure branch protection on main: require approved review and passing status checks before merge. Per Decision #5.

title: Update PROJECT_DECISIONS.md checkboxes for completed Vale items
description: Check off the two remaining TODO items in PROJECT_DECISIONS.md (Vale config and GitHub Actions CI) since Travis already implemented both.

title: Update README.md with project requirements and setup instructions
description: Document project dependencies and setup steps in README.md, including Dolt (database backend), beads (local issue tracking), Vale (prose linting), and any other tooling contributors need to get started.

title: Review: 2500 ChatGPT Conversations case study
description: Read and review https://aibuddy.software/papers/2500_chatgpt_conversations_case_study.pdf — found via decision loop, feels aligned with topics we want to write about. Research note in research/ai-conversation-analysis.md

title: Draft first article: Ethical guidelines for AI-human comparison discourse
description: Write the guidelines/rules-of-engagement article establishing how to talk responsibly about AI behaviors without pathologizing or harming disabled communities. This is the foundational piece that sets ground rules before the series explores deeper parallels. Tone: exploratory but careful — 'this is fascinating, but it could hurt people, so here's how we do it right.' Consider a memorable name for the guideline set (Asimov's laws inspiration). Publish alongside the intro article.

title: Guideline idea: Name the behavior, not the diagnosis
description: Lead idea for the guidelines article. When observing AI behaviors (looping, losing context, confabulating), describe what it's doing, not what it 'has.' Example: 'The model lost track of prior context' not 'The AI has dementia.' This is where the most harm happens and where accessibility expertise gives authority — Celeste knows how damaging casual diagnostic language is from working in the space where it has real consequences. Should be the sharpest, most quotable guideline.

title: Guideline idea: Accommodate the interaction, not the 'patient'
description: Second idea for the guidelines article. When something goes wrong in human-AI collaboration, the fix is environmental — change the prompt, restructure the task, reduce context noise. You're not treating the AI, you're adjusting the workspace. This maps directly to how accessibility actually works (fix the environment, not the person) and is a reframe most AI discourse hasn't made yet. That's where the thought leadership lives.

title: Guideline idea: Draw parallels at function level, never identity level
description: Third and hardest guideline for the article. Stop comparing AI to humans with disabilities entirely at the identity level. Even well-intentioned comparisons ('hallucination is like confabulation') flatten complex human experiences into convenient analogies. The rule: you can say 'both struggle with maintaining context over long interactions' without saying 'AI is like someone with X.' Draw parallels at the level of function, never identity. This is the hardest to follow, which is why it needs to be established upfront before the series goes deeper.
